transcript:
What you're looking at is a grid of 441 tiny cells, each one running its own little correction loop — numbers ticking inside them like a diagnostic readout nobody asked for. It starts calm, almost clinical, then the whole field ignites into orange and finally into a churning red-black mass that never settles.

Here's the part I love: the same operation runs the entire time. In the correction function, look at the multiplier — during convergence it's 0.91, quietly pulling rotation back toward zero. Later, in the overshoot phase, it becomes 1.08 — that's it, that's the whole difference between healing and destruction, just which side of one the number falls on. And each cell tracks its own "iteration" count, so cells don't break together — they break on a wave, staggered by a seed, like a system failing one component at a time while insisting each failure is just maintenance.

The numbers in each cell aren't decoration — they're the system's own readout of its rotation and scale, watching itself come apart in real time and calling it correction the whole way down.

cues.context: one running its own little correction loop — numbers ticking inside them
